blurb: Eighty short discourses in mixed prose and verse. These take the form of a simple prose narrative, which serves to frame an inspired saying, usually in verse. The udāna and dhammapada literature appear to overlap, and can be considered as one genre. The Dhammapada verses also have associated narratives, but these are found in the commentary. There are some indications that some of the narratives in the Udāna may be a little later than most of the material in the four main nikāyas.

children.blurb: This chapter begins with a series of discourses set immediately after the Buddha’s awakening, which are shared with the first chapter of the Khandhakas. It establishes the fundamental thesis of Buddhism: that awakening is possible through one’s own efforts. Each discourse deals with the idea of the true “brahmin”, defined not according to hereditary caste, but freedom from defilements.
The Buddha’s awakening has shown him a spiritual happiness that is better than any worldly happiness. This chapter focusses on the emotional dimension of enlightenment.
The discourses in this chapter focus on equanimity in the face of pleasure and pain. There are several examples of monastic conduct of poise and balance.
If a practitioner fails to achieve such balance, their undisciplined mind leads to harming themselves and others.
Broadening the focus from the individual practitioner, this chapter deals with the “all”, and shows how the Dhamma is inclusive of all beings, from kings to little fish.

children.translated_name: The Chapter on Blind From Birth
children.blurb: Returning to stories about the Buddha’s life, this chapter skips ahead to near the end. Meanwhile, it reminds us of those who have lost their way.
This chapter returns to the awakening of the Buddha, and employing water-based imagery, it reinforces the power of the Dhamma to carry us across the flood.

children.translated_name: The Chapter with the Pāṭali Villagers
children.blurb: The final chapter deals with the Buddha’s final passing away and the crisis of faith that this created. It begins with series of solemn declarations on Nibbana and ends with the spectacular passing of the monk Dabba.
